userManagement.title: User accounts
userManagement.summary: Create accounts, assign roles, and deactivate users.

user_list.title: Viewing all accounts
user_list.summary: All user accounts appear in this list. Use the search field and the role filter to narrow down to the account you need.
user_list.details: The list shows each user's name, email, role, and account status. Inactive accounts are grayed out. By default only active accounts are shown — toggle the filter to include inactive ones.

role_dropdown.title: Changing a user's role
role_dropdown.summary: Find the user and select a new role from the role dropdown. Role changes take effect immediately — the user's permissions update on their next action.
role_dropdown.details: Be deliberate when changing roles. Granting admin or super_admin access gives that user broad access to applicant data and system configuration.

deactivate.title: Deactivating a user
deactivate.summary: Deactivating a user prevents them from signing in. Their data and history are preserved. Click Reactivate on the same row to restore access.
deactivate.details: Deactivation is the preferred way to remove access for a staff member who has left. Hard-deletion is not available for accounts that have interacted with applicant data.

create_account.title: Creating a new account
create_account.summary: Click "+ Create Account" to add a staff member directly. Enter their name, email, and role. They receive a temporary password by email.
create_account.details: New accounts are active immediately. Ask the new staff member to sign in and change their password before their first session.

cant_modify_self.title: You cannot modify your own account
cant_modify_self.summary: For safety, you cannot change your own role or deactivate your own account from this page.
cant_modify_self.details: If you need to change your own access, ask another super-admin to make the change. This prevents accidental lock-outs.

admin_vs_super.question: What is the difference between admin and super_admin?
admin_vs_super.answer: Admins can review applications, manage documents, and access reports. Super admins can also manage user accounts, view the audit log, and change system configuration. Grant super_admin only to people who genuinely need that access.

deactivate_reversible.question: Is deactivation reversible?
deactivate_reversible.answer: Yes. Click Reactivate on the user's row to restore sign-in access immediately. All their data and history are intact.

inactive_accounts.message: {{count}} inactive account(s). Review periodically to confirm they are intentional.

auditLog.title: Audit log
auditLog.summary: Every sensitive action is recorded for HIPAA compliance.

what_logs.title: What gets recorded
what_logs.summary: The audit log records every sign-in, every access to protected health information, and every record change — who did it, when, and what changed.
what_logs.details: Searchable by user, action type, and date range. Use the filters to find specific events quickly.

compliance.title: HIPAA compliance requirement
compliance.summary: HIPAA requires that access to protected health information be logged and retained. This log satisfies that requirement.
compliance.details: The log cannot be disabled. If you notice gaps or errors in the log, contact your HIPAA compliance officer immediately.

investigate.title: Investigating access
investigate.summary: Use the user, action, and date filters together to investigate suspected unauthorized access or unusual activity.
investigate.details: For example, filter by a specific user and date range to see every action they took. Export the filtered results to share with your compliance team.

retention.title: Log retention
retention.summary: Audit log entries are kept for the legally required retention period and cannot be deleted by any user.
retention.details: The retention period is set by your organization's HIPAA policies. Contact your system administrator if you have questions about retention settings.

delete_entry.question: Can I delete an audit log entry?
delete_entry.answer: No. Audit entries are immutable — no user, including super-admins, can modify or delete them. This is a HIPAA requirement.

who_can_see.question: Who can see the audit log?
who_can_see.answer: Only super-admins have access to the audit log. Admins and other roles cannot view it.

use_filters.message: Use the filters above to investigate specific users, actions, or date ranges.

dashboard.title: Your super-admin dashboard
dashboard.summary: A full-system view: applications, sessions, user activity, and what needs your attention.

overview.title: System-wide stat cards
overview.summary: The stat cards show totals across all sessions and roles: total applications, pending reviews, accepted, and rejected.
overview.details: As a super-admin you see counts across the entire system, not just a single session. Use the session filter to narrow the view.

needs_attention.title: Needs Attention list
needs_attention.summary: The oldest pending applications appear here first. Longest-waiting applicants are surfaced so no one waits too long.
needs_attention.details: This list is the same one your admin team sees. You can jump to any application directly from here.

sessions_carousel.title: Session capacity cards
sessions_carousel.summary: Session cards show enrollment versus capacity with color warnings at 80% and 100%.
sessions_carousel.details: A red card means the session is full. You can adjust session capacity from the Sessions page.

user_management_link.title: User management shortcut
user_management_link.summary: Your dashboard includes a quick link to User Management so you can get there without navigating through the sidebar.
user_management_link.details: If you need to deactivate a user quickly, this shortcut saves a few clicks.

audit_log_link.title: Audit log shortcut
audit_log_link.summary: A direct link to the Audit Log appears on the super-admin dashboard for fast access to compliance records.
audit_log_link.details: Bookmark the audit log URL if you access it frequently for compliance reviews.

oldest_first.question: What does "oldest first" mean for Needs Attention?
oldest_first.answer: Applications are sorted by submission date with the earliest date at the top. This surfaces the applicants who have been waiting the longest so they are reviewed first.

red_session.question: Why is a session card red?
red_session.answer: The card turns red when enrolled applicants equal the session's total capacity. No new approved applicants can be added without increasing the capacity limit or waitlisting.

overdue_pending.message: {{count}} pending application(s); the oldest has waited {{days}} day(s).

all_caught_up.message: No pending applications. You are caught up.
